content.text: The systems specialization track focuses on the infrastructure that makes GenAI models fast, cheap, and reliable in production — inference serving, distributed training, model optimization, and deployment pipelines. Systems practitioners do not primarily create new model architectures; they make models run efficiently at scale. This track rewards engineers who find deep satisfaction in profiling, measuring, and optimizing — where a 20% latency reduction or a 2x throughput improvement is a concrete, quantifiable win.

The distinguishing skill of a systems practitioner is measurement discipline: the ability to instrument a system, identify where the bottleneck actually is (not where you think it is), and verify that a change improved the metric you care about. Systems work is empirical — assumptions about where time is spent are almost always wrong until you profile:
You do not optimize before measuring. Every optimization hypothesis is tested with a profiler, not intuition.
You can define and instrument the specific metrics that matter: time-to-first-token (TTFT), tokens per second per GPU, GPU memory utilization, cost per 1k tokens.
You understand how changes at one layer propagate through the stack. A batching change affects GPU memory, which affects quantization headroom, which affects model quality.
You know the memory bandwidth, compute capacity, and interconnect speed of the hardware you work on — and can calculate whether a bottleneck is compute-bound or memory-bound.

attrs.alt: GenAI systems stack: five layers from infrastructure to application, with three specialization profiles (Inference Engineer, Training Infrastructure, LLMOps/Platform, ML Compiler)

content.text: Inference engineering is the most accessible entry point for engineers with a software background. The goal is maximizing the tokens per second per dollar for a deployed model. The four levers in order of impact:

The largest single inference optimization for most teams is switching from naive single-request serving to continuous batching. vLLM implements PagedAttention, which manages the KV cache as paged memory and enables batching requests with different sequence lengths without wasting GPU memory:

# Naive serving (baseline — DO NOT do this in production):
from transformers import pipeline
pipe = pipeline("text-generation", model="meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct")

def serve_request(prompt: str) -> str:
    return pipe(prompt, max_new_tokens=512)[0]["generated_text"]
# Problem: processes one request at a time, GPU sits idle between requests

# vLLM continuous batching (3-10x throughput improvement):
from vllm import LLM, SamplingParams
from vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server import run_server

# Or use the CLI for production:
# vllm serve meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct \
#   --tensor-parallel-size 1 \
#   --gpu-memory-utilization 0.90 \
#   --max-num-seqs 256 \
#   --enable-chunked-prefill

# Benchmark before and after:
# Metric: requests/sec at p99 latency <= 3s
# Typical result: 5-8x throughput improvement over naive serving

Quantization reduces model weights from float16/bfloat16 to int8 or int4, cutting memory by 2–4x and often improving throughput because GPU memory bandwidth becomes less of a bottleneck. The tradeoff is quality:

# AWQ quantization (preserves quality better than GPTQ for most tasks):
from awq import AutoAWQForCausalLM
from transformers import AutoTokenizer

model_path = "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct"
quant_path  = "llama-3-8b-instruct-awq"

# Calibration dataset — use domain-representative data, not random text
calib_data = [
    "Explain the difference between RAG and fine-tuning.",
    "What are the tradeoffs of quantization for LLM inference?",
    # ... 128 examples from your actual use case
]

model = AutoAWQForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_path)
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_path)
model.quantize(tokenizer, quant_config={"zero_point": True, "q_group_size": 128, "w_bit": 4})
model.save_quantized(quant_path)

# Measure the quality delta before deploying:
# Run MMLU or your task benchmark on both versions
# Accept if quality degrades < 1-2% on your task metric
Every inference optimization should begin with a profiler. The common assumption — "the bottleneck is the forward pass" — is often wrong when caching, tokenization, or I/O dominates:

import torch
from torch.profiler import profile, ProfilerActivity, record_function

model = ...  # your loaded model

def profile_inference(prompt: str, n_tokens: int = 128):
    inputs = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt").to("cuda")

    with profile(
        activities=[ProfilerActivity.CPU, ProfilerActivity.CUDA],
        record_shapes=True,
        with_stack=True,
    ) as prof:
        with record_function("full_inference"):
            with torch.no_grad():
                output = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=n_tokens)

    # Print top 20 time consumers
    print(prof.key_averages().table(sort_by="cuda_time_total", row_limit=20))

    # Export to Chrome trace for visual inspection:
    prof.export_chrome_trace("inference_trace.json")
    # Open chrome://tracing and load the file

# What to look for:
# - attention ops dominating → try FlashAttention-2
# - memory transfers dominating → check batch size and KV cache config
# - tokenization in the hot path → batch tokenize, not per-request

Training infrastructure engineering focuses on making large model training fast, fault-tolerant, and cost-efficient across multiple GPUs and nodes. The entry point is understanding the three parallelism strategies and when each applies:
Model fits on 1 GPU; scale throughput

# PyTorch FSDP (Fully Sharded Data Parallel) — standard for 7-70B fine-tuning:
import torch
from torch.distributed.fsdp import FullyShardedDataParallel as FSDP
from torch.distributed.fsdp.wrap import transformer_auto_wrap_policy
from transformers.models.llama.modeling_llama import LlamaDecoderLayer
import functools

# Wrap the model: each transformer block is sharded independently
wrap_policy = functools.partial(
    transformer_auto_wrap_policy,
    transformer_layer_cls={LlamaDecoderLayer},
)

model = FSDP(
    model,
    auto_wrap_policy=wrap_policy,
    sharding_strategy=ShardingStrategy.FULL_SHARD,   # shard params + gradients + optimizer state
    mixed_precision=MixedPrecision(
        param_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
        reduce_dtype=torch.float32,
        buffer_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    ),
    device_id=torch.cuda.current_device(),
)

# Key metric to track: MFU (Model FLOP Utilization)
# MFU = actual FLOP/s / theoretical peak FLOP/s
# Good FSDP training: 40-55% MFU on A100
# Poor utilization (<30%): investigate communication overhead

def compute_mfu(model_params, tokens_per_sec, gpu_flops_per_sec):
    # 6 * params FLOPs per forward+backward pass per token
    flops_per_token = 6 * model_params
    actual_flops = flops_per_token * tokens_per_sec
    return actual_flops / gpu_flops_per_sec

# A100 80GB: 312 TFLOPS (bf16)
# Llama-3-8B: 8B params
# Good throughput: ~3,000 tokens/sec on 8xA100
mfu = compute_mfu(8e9, 3000, 312e12 * 8)
print(f"MFU: {mfu:.1%}")  # Target: >40%

Multi-day training runs on large GPU clusters fail — hardware failures, preemptions, and OOM errors are routine. A training infrastructure engineer must ensure training can resume without losing significant work:

# Checkpoint strategy for long training runs:
import os
from pathlib import Path

CHECKPOINT_STEPS = 500  # save every 500 steps
KEEP_LAST_N = 3         # delete older checkpoints to save disk

def save_checkpoint(model, optimizer, scheduler, step, loss, output_dir):
    ckpt_dir = Path(output_dir) / f"checkpoint-{step}"
    ckpt_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

    # For FSDP: use save_policy to shard checkpoint writing
    # (avoids CPU OOM when consolidating large model weights)
    from torch.distributed.fsdp import StateDictType
    with FSDP.state_dict_type(model, StateDictType.SHARDED_STATE_DICT):
        torch.save(model.state_dict(), ckpt_dir / "model.pt")

    torch.save({
        "step": step,
        "optimizer": optimizer.state_dict(),
        "scheduler": scheduler.state_dict(),
        "loss": loss,
    }, ckpt_dir / "training_state.pt")

    # Cleanup old checkpoints
    existing = sorted(Path(output_dir).glob("checkpoint-*"), key=lambda p: int(p.name.split("-")[1]))
    for old in existing[:-KEEP_LAST_N]:
        import shutil
        shutil.rmtree(old)

LLMOps engineers build the deployment pipelines, monitoring systems, and operational tooling that keep GenAI features reliable in production. This role overlaps with DevOps/SRE but requires specific understanding of how LLM outputs degrade over time.

# Minimal production deployment pipeline for a fine-tuned model:

# 1. Model registry: track versions and artifacts
# mlflow models register -m runs:/<run_id>/model -n "rag-reranker-v2"

# 2. Validation gate before deployment
# Run eval suite on candidate model:
python eval_suite.py \
  --model-path ./models/rag-reranker-v2 \
  --benchmark internal_eval_set \
  --min-score 0.87 \
  --compare-to production

# 3. Canary deployment (10% traffic → validate → 100%):
# In Kubernetes with Argo Rollouts:
# apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
# kind: Rollout
# spec:
#   strategy:
#     canary:
#       steps:
#       - setWeight: 10
#       - pause: {duration: 30m}  # observe metrics
#       - setWeight: 100

# 4. Monitoring: key signals to alert on
# - p99 TTFT > 5s (latency regression)
# - output_length drops > 20% (model producing shorter outputs than expected)
# - refusal_rate > 5% (model over-refusing)
# - error_rate > 0.1% (serving failures)

LLM output quality drifts for reasons unrelated to model changes: input distribution shifts as users send different prompts, upstream data changes affect RAG retrieval, and context length distributions change with new features. Detecting drift requires monitoring both inputs and outputs:
Systems work is measured in numbers. A portfolio on this track looks different from a research or product portfolio — it is benchmarks, before/after comparisons, and architecture diagrams:

Document a serving optimization: baseline measurement → hypothesis → implementation → result. "Switched from HuggingFace pipeline to vLLM: 6.2x throughput improvement at p99 latency of 2.1s on A10G." This is a complete systems story.
vLLM, TGI, and lm-evaluation-harness all accept PRs. A merged PR in a high-visibility inference framework is a strong signal because reviewers who merged it are well-known in the field.

A blog post comparing quantization methods with measured quality/speed tradeoffs on a specific model and task is highly useful to the community and demonstrates the measurement discipline that systems roles require.
A public Kubernetes deployment manifest for vLLM with autoscaling, health checks, and proper resource requests is a concrete artifact that shows operational thinking.

# Example systems portfolio entry (benchmark analysis post structure):

Title: "AWQ vs GPTQ vs GGUF for Llama-3-8B on A10G: A Measured Comparison"

Sections:
1. Experimental setup
   - Hardware: A10G 24GB, Ubuntu 22.04, CUDA 12.1
   - Model: Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct (bf16 baseline)
   - Quantization tools: AutoAWQ 0.2.0, AutoGPTQ 0.7.1, llama.cpp b3xxx
   - Quality benchmark: MMLU (5-shot), HellaSwag, your internal task

2. Results table (benchmark is the artifact)
   | Method | Memory (GB) | Tokens/sec | MMLU | HellaSwag |
   |--------|-------------|------------|------|-----------|
   | bf16   | 16.0        | 42         | 68.4 | 82.1      |
   | AWQ 4-bit | 4.8     | 94         | 67.1 | 81.3      |
   | GPTQ 4-bit | 4.9    | 88         | 66.8 | 80.9      |

3. Recommendation
   - "AWQ is the better default for 4-bit due to X..."
   - "GGUF preferred when deploying on CPU..."

4. Reproduction instructions (the most important section)
   - GitHub repo with exact commands
   - Docker image for reproducibility

The systems specialization track encompasses inference engineering (serving latency and throughput), training infrastructure (distributed training efficiency), and LLMOps (deployment pipelines and quality monitoring). The core discipline is measurement: profiling before optimizing, defining specific metrics (TTFT, MFU, cost per token), and verifying results rigorously. Entry points are accessible — switching a naive serving setup to vLLM and documenting the throughput improvement is a concrete, measurable first contribution. The systems track compounds through tool depth: practitioners who deeply understand one layer of the inference stack (serving, quantization, or hardware scheduling) become the people others consult when optimization becomes urgent.
